chapters.title: Bargain yourself a fortune
chapters.text:
Compassion is the first social skill parents must teach their children. Mutual understanding is a fundamental value in our society. However, such sympathy can be confused with deceit. As a community, we seek personal benefit through others. But it does not have to be cynical; this is when empathy enters the stage.

Having empathy means not only understanding others' feelings but also seeing beyond the smile. A genuine commitment to another person helps them trust us more. An experiment on neural resonance found that good listeners can predict each others' responses. We can exercise our resonance skills by observing speakers or watching TV interviews.

Labeling emotions results from good observation and is another valuable tool for appealing to people. Talking about negative feelings helps relieve related stress. When we name the worry or fear, we also show solidarity with the other person. “It seems like you're worried about that job interview” or “It looks like this person really stresses you out” are good examples of labeling. Conversely, starting a conversation with: “I think you…” is a bad tactic, as it focuses on our opinion instead of the person's circumstances.

Another benefit of branding emotions is neutralizing the negative ones. The latter consists of two levels: emotional cover and actual feelings. A crabby aunt who feels alone at family gatherings is familiar to everyone. Labeling her unhappiness will soothe her. To condense the lesson, negate pessimism with positivity.

Quitting after a refusal is a waste of possibilities.

The third way to establish rapport is to see “no” not as a refusal but as a need for further dialogue. We dread rejection, but it has something that “yes” cannot provide. Most of the time, we focus on our words instead of the opponent. That's why they say “yes” to get rid of us. On the flip side, hearing “no” promises more trust. When accepting a refusal, we express our readiness to listen to the alternative. The need for control and autonomy is natural to people, so a refusal often means:

• I'm not ready to agree.
• I don't understand.
• I need something different.
• I can't afford it.
• I need to know more.

In other words, “no” gives opportunities to approach the person differently.

chapters.title: Kill them with kindness
chapters.text:
Optimists may say love and understanding move our society. Pessimists may challenge them, saying that profit drives the world. Both sides are correct, but the truth is always in the middle, as we know. More specifically, we should seek benefit through empathy.

As a form of communication, negotiation is as old as humankind itself. If technological progress could be words, it would be bargaining. Most interactions, whether with our boss or toddler, resemble a game of ping-pong to get what we want. “I want a raise” darts in the minds of many office workers daily. Yet, we're so afraid of negotiating because it feels like arguing. We must remove the fear of bargaining or remain unsatisfied.

Humans are illogical: Daniel Kahneman, a ground-breaking psychologist, proved this idea through years of research. He found two ways people guide their thoughts: animalistic and logical. Kahneman argued that our feelings and desires deeply affect our judgment. A sound mind often blurs with confusion, anger, or excitement. As a result, we fall into the trap of emotions.

Fortunately, we can use this fact to our benefit. His FBI experience proved to Chris Voss that most negotiations resemble surges of emotions. People seek community due to their social nature — the desire to be understood is innate to everyone. When they feel heard, people are more likely to lower their guard.

Comfort comes from similarities between people.

Mirroring is a part of being a good listener, which comes from the natural urge to soothe each other. Subconsciously copying our crush's mannerisms or speech patterns to achieve reciprocity is a good example.

The main idea of mirroring is repeating the other person's question or statement. Suppose your old-fashioned boss asks for a handwritten report. You can mirror their request: “I'm sorry, a handwritten report?” as if calling for clarification. The repeated question allows them to hear their explanation again, recalibrating the task. Chances are they'll either change the task or do it themselves.

Other secrets to persuasiveness include:

• Using a soothing, cheerful, assertive voice, depending on the situation.
• Focusing on a person's words instead of building an argument.
• Slowing down the pace of conversation to help the other person feel heard.
• Smiling to ease the tension.

chapters.title: Take a walk in someone else’s shoes
chapters.text:
Compassion is the first social skill parents must teach their children. Mutual understanding is a fundamental value in our society. However, such sympathy can be confused with deceit. As a community, we seek personal benefit through others. But it does not have to be cynical; this is when empathy enters the stage.

An experiment on neural resonance found that good listeners can predict each others' responses. We can exercise our resonance skills by observing speakers or watching TV interviews.

A crabby aunt who feels alone at family gatherings is familiar to everyone. Labeling her unhappiness will soothe her. To condense the lesson, negate pessimism with positivity.

chapters.title: Always bring your thread when entering the negotiation maze
chapters.text:
The word “negotiation” has a weirdly dangerous connotation. Only minotaurs of business seem to step into the maze of bargaining. However, the rules and nuances of negotiation stem from human psychology. We require a self-made thread to lead us straight to the opponent's heart.

Before we enter the maze, there are two rules: we must persuade others, not ourselves, and never compromise. Empathy is a powerful, but not exclusive, tool to achieve them. Here are a few others:

1. Pausing encourages the other side to exhaust their thinking and give us more information.
2. Showing engagement through fillers like “Uh-huh,” “I understand,” and “OK.”
3. Paraphrasing the opponent's words to confirm our understanding
4. Summarizing their statement to show we're on the same page.

These tactics aim to lead the other person to say “That's right” instead of “You're right,” as if to end the invasive conversation without believing our arguments. This strategy is useful when negotiation comes to nothing as it ensures that both parties appreciate each other's values and motivations and are willing to look for common ground.

No change has ever come without a bit of gambling.

Another thing we care about while negotiating is fairness. When accused of unfairness, we immediately try to disprove it by doing whatever it takes, including agreeing to another person’s terms. Do the same to your opponent, and see them raise the offer to justify themselves.

At the same time, we must remember the value of persuasiveness. When negotiating a price or a business deal, make sure to:

• Lower your opponent’s expectations: You avoid loss if it's more likely than success. Afraid to take a risk, we prioritize any profit over losing everything.
• Let your opponent offer first: You need all possible information to negotiate the price confidently.
• Give a range: People subconsciously choose the lower number (for example, $130,000-$170,000).
• Give an odd number: Using this tactic makes it look calculated and thus respected.
• Discuss non-monetary terms if your financial ones don't yet match: If necessary, offer your opponent the same to keep good relationships.
• Offer a gift to start on a good note: But don't use it as a tip or it's a bribe.

chapters.title: Don’t get too emotional
chapters.text:
Emotions can be allies and foes. For example, fear negates our arguments even before we begin the conversation. One unspoken rule of negotiating is never to ask first, as it always places a “debt,” thus losing to our opponent already. When we hurry to state our terms, we lose the opportunity to win by falling victim to our fear (and pride).

However, the other person has emotions, too. As bargainers, we can grab this questionable part of the human psyche by its tail and use it to our advantage. Tuning into our feelings is necessary to avoid sounding harsh or suspicious. The trick is to balance our emotions with calibrated negotiation.

Kindness is good for breaking the ice, but calmness is excellent for sealing the deal.

If signing a deal or cutting the rent is the goal, we must learn to influence our future partners or landlords. We can try the following techniques:

• Open-ended questions, especially “How?” and “What?” “What made you do it?” and “How should I do it?” force the opponent to solve the problem they imposed on you and avoid escalation.
• Coax all the negotiators. There's never one person to make a decision. Asking, “How do your colleagues/family view the situation?” or “What effect does it have on your team?” will show your care and empathy.
• Spot nervous negotiators or liars when they switch to third-person pronouns and speak in complicated sentences.
• Incite reverse empathy. Use your name (introduce yourself) in a conversation to unwind it.
• Cement deals with three Yeses from the other person. Agreeing to commit is the first “yes,” labeling and summarizing their point is the second, and the “how” or “what” question is the final yes.

According to research, words convey only 7% of the message, while voice and body language convey 38% and 55%, respectively. When the verbal message doesn't align with the physical expression, we fail to convince the other person. To successfully negotiate, we must learn to control our emotions, especially fear, anger, and worry. We risk gaining a bad reputation or blowing the deal if we don't. A calm and neutral attitude is best for negotiating.

He who has learned to disagree without being disagreeable has discovered the most valuable secret of negotiation. ~ Robert Estabrook

Did you know? People with brain damage in areas that control emotions visibly struggle to make decisions. Emotions help us to choose without logical overwhelm.

chapters.title: Get to know your opponent the way you know yourself
chapters.text:
Life is unpredictable, and so are bargains. Similar to emotions, our prior knowledge impacts the final results. It is frustrating to learn a vital fact after an important meeting. However, such surprises teach us flexibility in any situation.

Three types of effective leverage can be used in negotiations:

1. Positive: The other person gives us power by expressing their want of what we can provide.
2. Negative: Pressure based on threats exploits the opponent's fear of loss.
3. Normative: This is using the opponent's values and standards to gain an advantage.

Positive and normative types are more ethical and, therefore, preferred. What's common for all three is research: What does your opponent want to achieve, and what can't they lose? Who is their average client? What is their reputation?

Some other ways to seal the deal include:

• Shared interests or values. Psychologically, we accept those who are similar to us.
• Showing interest in others' passions or goals to earn trust.
• Reasoning our demands by using “because” for empathy.
• Engaging in face-to-face meetings for more insight.

Some people are Black Swans, unpredictable and sometimes extreme. Labeling them “crazy” is easier than trying to understand them. However, their “craziness” may come from being ill-informed, restricted, or having other goals. We should learn to carefully observe others' speech and expressions through dialogue. Seeing our counterpart's troubles must be intuitive, so empathy is critical.

Some people have difficulties managing their emotions. Being gentle, forceful, or persuasive requires lots of self-control. Our negotiation style depends on emotions, so mastering a bargain means complete management of our mental abilities.

Voss classifies three debating styles:

1. Analyst
2. Accommodator
3. Assertive

Analysts are short on emotions. Structure and skepticism are their main values, so it's better to get straight down to business. They prefer quality over time, so their analysis is often thorough. However, “How” and “What” questions baffle them.

Accommodators are open and friendly. Unlike Analysts, who need quiet time to think, they are chatty and fear silence. So, one can annoy the other by talking too much. Those who accommodate during talks don't usually gain a lot and need more firmness.

Assertive people are just that. But they care only about business and thus can be aggressive. Knowing our opponent’s style can show us things to improve, so we can confidently enter the next bargain.

Search for a deal somewhere between assertion and goodwill.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Being a perfect negotiator seems impossible, but its secrets hide in plain sight. In fact, we can find them in our minds if we know where to look.

Business people may want us to stay oblivious, but their skills have the same source as ours. No one can steal something made by the whole of humankind. Proper contact requires natural skills like empathy, understanding, attention, and kindness. Ironically, society has portrayed negotiation as the aggressive tossing of demands. But we don't have to be afraid of debates. They resemble a psychological game, and the only threat is our anxiety.

Empathy is great for appeasing others. There are numerous tools to enhance rapport, like paying attention to opponents’ words, sharing their values or interests, and soothing worry. These tools can break the ice and help us gain mutual benefit. On the other hand, not all people are naturally open to the art of negotiation. Too much assertiveness or vigor can be harmful. But even sharp tongues want to be heard. Understanding and observation work even with those who think they're invincible. Mirroring and paraphrasing their offerings, giving them time to think, and showing genuine interest can win the deal with any self-assured counterpart.

Sometimes, it's us who are too assertive. Bargaining can be relentless, especially when we need to fight the fear of arguing. However, too much force can have negative consequences. Therefore, minding our emotions and sensing the other side’s feelings is a valuable skill to practice.

Try this


1. Practice your arguments: Consider your wants, reasoning, and what your opponent may require from the deal.
2. Prepare beforehand: Investigate your opponent's needs, pressures, and future options. It will help avoid surprises.
3. Play a broken record: Repeat your position when there's pressure on you.
